lines.greek: φάρμακον ἀνδροφόνον διζήμενος, ὄφρα οἱ εἴη
ἰοὺς χρίεσθαι χαλκήρεας· ἀλλʼ ὁ μὲν οὔ οἱ
δῶκεν, ἐπεί ῥα θεοὺς νεμεσίζετο αἰὲν ἐόντας,
ἀλλὰ πατήρ οἱ δῶκεν ἐμός· φιλέεσκε γὰρ αἰνῶς—
τοῖος ἐὼν μνηστῆρσιν ὁμιλήσειεν Ὀδυσσεύς·
πάντες κʼ ὠκύμοροί τε γενοίατο πικρόγαμοί τε.
ἀλλʼ ἦ τοι μὲν ταῦτα θεῶν ἐν γούνασι κεῖται,
ἤ κεν νοστήσας ἀποτίσεται, ἦε καὶ οὐκί,
οἷσιν ἐνὶ μεγάροισι· σὲ δὲ φράζεσθαι ἄνωγα,
ὅππως κε μνηστῆρας ἀπώσεαι ἐκ μεγάροιο.
εἰ δʼ ἄγε νῦν ξυνίει καὶ ἐμῶν ἐμπάζεο μύθων·
αὔριον εἰς ἀγορὴν καλέσας ἥρωας Ἀχαιοὺς
μῦθον πέφραδε πᾶσι, θεοὶ δʼ ἐπὶ μάρτυροι ἔστων.
μνηστῆρας μὲν ἐπὶ σφέτερα σκίδνασθαι ἄνωχθι,
μητέρα δʼ, εἴ οἱ θυμὸς ἐφορμᾶται γαμέεσθαι,
ἂψ ἴτω ἐς μέγαρον πατρὸς μέγα δυναμένοιο·
οἱ δὲ γάμον τεύξουσι καὶ ἀρτυνέουσιν ἔεδνα
πολλὰ μάλʼ, ὅσσα ἔοικε φίλης ἐπὶ παιδὸς ἕπεσθαι.
σοὶ δʼ αὐτῷ πυκινῶς ὑποθήσομαι, αἴ κε πίθηαι·
νῆʼ ἄρσας ἐρέτῃσιν ἐείκοσιν, ἥ τις ἀρίστη,

interlinear: a-drug man-slaying seeking, so-that for-him there-might-be
arrows to-smear, bronze-tipped; but he not to-him
gave-it, since indeed the-gods he-feared, the-ever-existing,
but father to-him gave-it, mine; for-he-loved-him exceedingly).
such being, with-the-wooers might-Odysseus mingle;
all would swift-fated become, and bitter-in-their-wooing.
but truly indeed these-things on-the-gods' knees lie,
whether returning he-will-take-vengeance, or also not,
in-his-own halls. but-you to-consider I-bid,
how the-wooers you-may-thrust-out from the-hall.
come now, understand, and of-my words take-heed:
tomorrow to an-assembly having-called the-hero Achaeans,
your-word declare to-all, and-let-the-gods be witnesses.
the-wooers to their-own-homes to-scatter bid,
and-your-mother, if her heart urges-her to-marry,
back let-her-go to the-hall of-her-father, greatly-powerful;
and-they a-marriage will-make and will-prepare bride-gifts
very many, as-many-as befits a-dear daughter to-follow.
and-to-you yourself shrewdly I-will-give-counsel, if you-will-heed:
a-ship having-fitted with-rowers twenty, whichever is-best,
literal: seeking a deadly drug, that he might have it
to smear on his bronze-tipped arrows; but Ilus would not
give it to him, since he feared the gods who are forever,
but my father gave it, for he loved him exceedingly).
Such a man, if he could meet the wooers,
would make them all swift to die and bitter in their wooing.
But truly these things lie upon the knees of the gods,
whether he shall return and take vengeance, or not,
within his own halls. But I bid you consider
how you may thrust the wooers out of the hall.
Come now, mark me, and take heed of my words:
tomorrow call the Achaean warriors to an assembly
and declare your word to all, and let the gods be witnesses.
Bid the wooers scatter to their own homes,
and as for your mother, if her heart urges her to marry,
let her go back to the hall of her father, a man of great power;
and they will make the marriage and prepare bride-gifts,
very many, as befits a dear daughter to take with her.
And to you yourself I will give shrewd counsel, if you will heed it:
fit out a ship with twenty rowers, the best that there is,
translations.elizabethan:
He had gone thither in his swift black ship
to seek a poison for his bronze-tipped shafts,
a drug to kill with. Ilus would not give it,
fearing the gods that live for ever; yet
my father gave it, loving him so well.
Let such a man as that come face to face
with these thy suitors, and they all would find
a quick death and a bitter wedding day.
But these things rest upon the knees of gods,
whether he comes at last and takes revenge
within his halls, or never comes at all.
Meanwhile, do thou consider how to drive
these suitors from thy hall. Now mark me well
and lay my words to heart. At break of day
call all the Achaean warriors to assembly,
speak out thy word to them, and let the gods
be witness to it. Bid the suitors go,
each to his own. And for thy mother, if
her heart is set on marrying again,
let her go back to her great father's house.
There shall they make the match and set the gifts,
as many as befit a daughter dear.
And to thyself I give this shrewd advice,
if thou wilt hear it: fit a ship with twenty
oarsmen, the best of any thou canst find.
translations.modernist:
He had gone there in his fast ship to find a poison for his bronze arrowheads. Something that kills. Ilus would not give it to him. He was afraid of the gods, who go on forever. My father gave it to him. He was very fond of him.

Let a man like that walk in on your suitors. They would all die quickly and their courting would turn bitter.

But that rests on the knees of the gods. He may come back and settle it in his own house. He may not.

Your business is different. Work out how to get these men out of your hall. Listen to me now and do what I say. Tomorrow morning call the Achaean warriors to an assembly. Say your piece to all of them. Let the gods hear it.

Tell the suitors to go home, every one to his own place. As for your mother, if she has a mind to marry, send her back to her father's house. He is a powerful man. They will arrange the match there and set out the bride-gifts, as many as a man gives for a daughter he loves.

And here is my advice to you, if you will take it. Fit out a ship with twenty oarsmen, the best one you can get.
translations.storybook:
He had sailed there in his swift ship to look for a poison to smear on his bronze arrows, the kind that kills a man. But Ilus would not give him any, because he was frightened of the gods, who live for ever and ever. So my father gave it to him instead, for he loved him dearly.

Now, if a man like that were to walk in among your suitors, every one of them would die quickly, and their courting would turn very bitter indeed!

But whether he comes home and settles with them in his own hall, or never comes home at all, that lies upon the knees of the gods.

What you must do is this. Think how to get these men out of your house. Listen carefully now, and do exactly as I say. Tomorrow morning, call all the Achaean warriors together to an assembly, and say your piece to every one of them, and let the gods themselves listen as witnesses.

Tell the suitors to go home, each one to his own house. And as for your mother, if she truly wishes to marry again, let her go back to her father's house, for he is a powerful man. They will arrange the wedding there, and set out all the bride-gifts that a father gives for a daughter he loves.

And here is my own advice for you, if you will take it: get a ship ready, the very best you can find, with twenty oarsmen.

murray.text: Would, I say, that in such strength Odysseus might come amongst the wooers; then should they all find swift destruction and bitterness in their wooing. Yet these things verily lie on the knees of the gods, whether he shall return and wreak vengeance in his halls, or whether he shall not; but for thyself, I bid thee take thought

how thou mayest thrust forth the wooers from the hall. Come now, give ear, and hearken to my words. On the morrow call to an assembly the Achaean lords, and speak out thy word to all, and let the gods be thy witnesses. As for the wooers, bid them scatter, each to his own;

and for thy mother, if her heart bids her marry, let her go back to the hall of her mighty father, and there they will prepare a wedding feast, and make ready the gifts full many—aye, all that should follow after a well-loved daughter. And to thyself will I give wise counsel, if thou wilt hearken.

Man with twenty rowers the best ship thou hast, and go to seek tidings of thy father, that has long been gone, if haply any mortal may tell thee, or thou mayest hear a voice from Zeus, which oftenest brings tidings to men. First go to Pylos and question goodly Nestor,
